fragments.content: Cushing syndrome results from chronic exposure to excess glucocorticoids. Etiologies: (1) Exogenous (most common) - iatrogenic from therapeutic glucocorticoid administration; (2) Endogenous ACTH-dependent (80-85%): Cushing disease (pituitary ACTH-secreting adenoma, ~70%), ectopic ACTH secretion (small cell lung cancer, bronchial carcinoids, other neuroendocrine tumors); (3) Endogenous ACTH-independent: adrenal adenoma/carcinoma, primary bilateral macronodular adrenal hyperplasia, primary pigmented nodular adrenocortical disease. Clinical features: central obesity, moon facies, dorsocervical fat pad (buffalo hump), proximal muscle weakness, wide purple striae (>1cm), easy bruising, osteoporosis, hypertension, glucose intolerance/diabetes, hirsutism, menstrual irregularities, mood changes. Complications: cardiovascular disease, infections, thromboembolism, fractures.

Treatment depends on etiology. For exogenous: gradual taper of glucocorticoid if clinically feasible. Cushing disease (pituitary): transsphenoidal surgical resection first-line (remission 60-90% for microadenomas); repeat surgery or radiation therapy for persistent/recurrent disease; medical therapy as bridge or long-term if surgery contraindicated/unsuccessful. Ectopic ACTH: resect tumor if localized; if metastatic, medical cortisol suppression plus oncologic treatment. Adrenal causes: unilateral adrenalectomy for adenoma/carcinoma; bilateral adrenalectomy for bilateral hyperplasia (requires lifelong glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid replacement). Medical therapies inhibit cortisol synthesis: ketoconazole (hepatotoxicity monitoring), metyrapone, osilodrostat, mitotane (adrenal carcinoma); mifepristone (glucocorticoid receptor antagonist) for hyperglycemia control. Pasireotide (somatostatin analog) for Cushing disease. Bilateral adrenalectomy definitive but causes permanent adrenal insufficiency and risk of Nelson syndrome (pituitary tumor growth).

Diagnostic approach: First confirm hypercortisolism with one of four tests (all have false positives/negatives): (1) Late-night salivary cortisol (×2) - elevated; (2) 24-hour urinary free cortisol (×2-3) - elevated; (3) 1mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test - morning cortisol >1.8 mcg/dL indicates abnormal suppression; (4) Midnight serum cortisol - elevated. If confirmed, measure plasma ACTH to determine ACTH-dependence: suppressed (<5 pg/mL) indicates ACTH-independent (adrenal source); normal/elevated indicates ACTH-dependent. For ACTH-dependent: high-dose dexamethasone suppression test (Cushing disease usually suppresses, ectopic doesn't) and/or CRH stimulation test; pituitary MRI; if discordant, inferior petrosal sinus sampling (gold standard to distinguish pituitary vs ectopic). For ACTH-independent: adrenal CT/MRI. Screen for complications: bone density, glucose, lipids, blood pressure.
